news_summary: Recent advancements in understanding aging-related diseases, innovative protein design targeting key receptors, the integration of AI to enhance psychedelic drug research, and intensified Ebola response efforts in DR Congo highlight significant progress in health science and disease management.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes avancées dans la compréhension des maladies liées au vieillissement, la conception innovante de protéines ciblant des récepteurs clés, l'intégration de l'intelligence artificielle pour améliorer la recherche sur les psychédéliques, ainsi que l'intensification des efforts de lutte contre Ebola en République démocratique du Congo témoignent des progrès significatifs réalisés dans le domaine des sciences de la santé et de la prise en charge des maladies.
news_summary_es: Los recientes avances en la comprensión de las enfermedades relacionadas con el envejecimiento, el diseño innovador de proteínas dirigidas a receptores clave, la integración de la inteligencia artificial para mejorar la investigación sobre fármacos psicodélicos y la intensificación de los esfuerzos de respuesta al ébola en la República Democrática del Congo ponen de relieve los importantes avances logrados en las ciencias de la salud y el tratamiento de las enfermedades.

articles.title: Scientists discover a two-stage aging process that may cause cancer and arthritis
articles.summary: A new theory suggests many age-related diseases may actually start decades before symptoms appear. Researchers say early-life damage — from infections, injuries, or genetic mutations — can remain hidden until aging weakens the body’s ability to keep it under control. This could explain why conditions like cancer, osteoarthritis, and shingles suddenly emerge later in life.
articles.content:
Researchers are offering a new way to understand why aging is so closely connected to chronic illness. In a review published in Aging-US titled "Aging as a multifactorial disorder with two stages," scientists from University College London and Queen Mary University of London describe a model suggesting that diseases linked to aging may develop through two separate but connected phases over the course of life.

The review was written by David Gems and Alexander Carver from University College London, along with Yuan Zhao from Queen Mary University of London. Their work combines ideas from evolutionary biology with findings from modern biomedical research to explain how early damage in the body may later contribute to diseases such as cancer, arthritis, and infections.

How Early-Life Damage May Shape Health Decades Later

According to the researchers, the first stage begins earlier in life when the body experiences various forms of disruption. These can include infections, physical injuries, or genetic mutations. While the body is often able to repair or contain much of this damage, some of it may remain hidden rather than being fully removed.

The second stage occurs later in life as normal genetic activity starts changing in ways that are no longer beneficial to the body. These late-life biological changes can weaken the body's ability to keep earlier damage under control. As a result, previously contained problems may gradually develop into disease.

The scientists argue that this process helps explain why many illnesses appear mainly in older adults even though their origins may trace back much earlier.

Why Diseases Like Shingles and Arthritis Appear With Age

The review highlights aging as a multifactorial process, meaning it is driven by many interacting biological factors instead of a single cause. The proposed model suggests that the combination of earlier damage and later-life genetic changes plays a major role in age-related disease.

For example, dormant viruses that remain inactive for years can become active again when the immune system weakens with age, leading to conditions such as shingles. In a similar way, injuries sustained in youth may eventually contribute to osteoarthritis as aging tissues become less resilient over time.

Inherited genetic mutations may also stay silent for decades before increasing the risk of diseases such as cancer or fibrosis later in life.

Evolutionary Biology and Aging Research

The researchers say their model builds on long-standing evolutionary theories of aging. One influential idea is that natural selection becomes weaker later in life, allowing harmful biological processes to emerge with age because they have less impact on reproduction and survival earlier in life.

The review also references studies involving the roundworm Caenorhabditis elegans. In these experiments, early mechanical damage in the worms eventually led to fatal infections in old age. The scientists suggest similar patterns may also occur in humans.

A New Framework for Healthier Aging

Overall, the review presents aging as a process shaped by multiple interacting causes that unfold over time. By separating aging into two major stages, early-life damage and later-life genetic activity, the researchers believe their framework could help guide future strategies aimed at disease prevention and healthier aging.

The findings also raise the possibility that reducing damage earlier in life or targeting harmful late-life biological changes could help lower the risk of chronic disease in older adults.
articles.reasoning: The article presents a significant scientific advancement in understanding aging and its connection to chronic diseases such as cancer and arthritis. This new two-stage aging model offers a meaningful framework that could guide future prevention and treatment strategies, potentially benefiting a broad population by improving health outcomes in aging. The research is well-contextualized, detailed, and focused on a single impactful topic.
articles.personality_title: Scientists uncover two-stage aging process linked to cancer and arthritis
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied why diseases like cancer and arthritis mostly affect older people. New research suggests these diseases might actually start much earlier in life, but only show symptoms later.

**What happened** – Researchers from University College London and Queen Mary University of London proposed a new model explaining aging in two stages. The first stage involves damage from infections, injuries, or genetic changes that happen early in life but stay hidden. The second stage occurs later when the body’s natural defenses weaken, allowing this earlier damage to cause diseases.

**Impact** – This two-stage idea helps explain why diseases such as shingles, arthritis, and cancer appear suddenly in older age even though the causes started decades before. For example, viruses dormant in the body can reactivate when the immune system weakens, or old injuries can lead to arthritis as tissues lose strength. This new understanding could change how we prevent and treat age-related diseases.

**What's next step** – Scientists hope this model will guide future research to find ways to reduce early damage or strengthen the body’s defenses later in life. This could lead to better strategies for preventing or delaying chronic diseases as people get older.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new two-stage aging model explains how early-life damage and later-life body changes together cause many diseases that appear in old age.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent un processus de vieillissement en deux étapes lié au cancer et à l'arthrite
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent depuis longtemps à comprendre pourquoi des maladies comme le cancer et l'arthrite touchent surtout les personnes âgées. Une nouvelle recherche suggère que ces maladies pourraient en fait commencer bien plus tôt dans la vie, mais ne montrer des symptômes que plus tard.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'University College London et de la Queen Mary University of London ont proposé un nouveau modèle expliquant le vieillissement en deux étapes. La première étape concerne les dommages causés par des infections, blessures ou changements génétiques qui se produisent tôt dans la vie mais restent cachés. La deuxième étape survient plus tard quand les défenses naturelles du corps s'affaiblissent, permettant à ces dommages anciens de causer des maladies.

**Impact** – Cette idée en deux étapes aide à comprendre pourquoi des maladies comme le zona, l'arthrite et le cancer apparaissent soudainement chez les personnes âgées, même si leurs causes ont commencé des décennies plus tôt. Par exemple, des virus dormants peuvent se réactiver lorsque le système immunitaire faiblit, ou des blessures anciennes peuvent provoquer de l'arthrite quand les tissus deviennent moins résistants. Cette nouvelle compréhension pourrait changer la prévention et le traitement des maladies liées à l'âge.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques espèrent que ce modèle guidera de futures recherches pour réduire les dommages précoces ou renforcer les défenses du corps plus tard dans la vie. Cela pourrait mener à de meilleures stratégies pour prévenir ou retarder les maladies chroniques chez les personnes âgées.

**Phrase clé** – Un nouveau modèle de vieillissement en deux étapes explique comment les dommages précoces et les changements corporels tardifs causent ensemble de nombreuses maladies apparaissant à un âge avancé.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren un proceso de envejecimiento en dos etapas relacionado con el cáncer y la artritis
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, los científicos han estudiado por qué enfermedades como el cáncer y la artritis afectan principalmente a personas mayores. Una nueva investigación sugiere que estas enfermedades podrían comenzar mucho antes en la vida, pero solo muestran síntomas más tarde.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de University College London y Queen Mary University of London propusieron un nuevo modelo que explica el envejecimiento en dos etapas. La primera etapa implica daños por infecciones, lesiones o cambios genéticos que ocurren temprano en la vida pero permanecen ocultos. La segunda etapa sucede más tarde cuando las defensas naturales del cuerpo se debilitan, permitiendo que esos daños antiguos causen enfermedades.

**Impacto** – Esta idea de dos etapas ayuda a entender por qué enfermedades como el herpes zóster, la artritis y el cáncer aparecen de repente en la vejez, aunque sus causas comenzaron décadas antes. Por ejemplo, virus dormidos pueden reactivarse cuando el sistema inmunológico se debilita, o lesiones antiguas pueden causar artritis a medida que los tejidos pierden fuerza. Esta nueva comprensión podría cambiar cómo prevenimos y tratamos las enfermedades relacionadas con la edad.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos esperan que este modelo guíe futuras investigaciones para reducir el daño temprano o fortalecer las defensas del cuerpo en etapas tardías. Esto podría llevar a mejores estrategias para prevenir o retrasar enfermedades crónicas en personas mayores.

**Frase clave** – Un nuevo modelo de envejecimiento en dos etapas explica cómo el daño temprano y los cambios corporales tardíos juntos causan muchas enfermedades que aparecen en la vejez.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate, warm-toned painting of a young, vibrant tree with visible small cracks and faint glowing marks on its bark symbolizing early-life damage, gradually transitioning into an older, sturdier tree whose branches subtly transform into delicate, branching DNA strands and softly glowing cellular patterns, illustrating late-life genetic changes and the unfolding of age-related diseases.

articles.title: De novo design of miniproteins targeting GPCRs
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 21 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10656-8De novo design of miniproteins targeting GPCRs
articles.content: Supplementary Information file contains Supplementary Figs. 1-69, Supplementary Tables 1-11 and Supplementary References. Supplementary Figs. 1-6. Scaffold diversity of metaproteome-derived miniproteins scaffolds, design principles and computational analysis of MetaGen and RFdiffusion designs. Supplementary Figs. 7-9. OPS-RD assay development and optimization. Supplementary Figs. 10-15. Discovery, optimization, and characterization of MRGPRX1 binders. Supplementary Figs. 16-19. NK1R binder screening and pharmacological characterization. Supplementary Figs. 20-21. Cryo-EM structures of MRGPRX1-miniprotein complexes. Supplementary Figs. 22-25. CXCR4 binder screening and characterization. Supplementary Figs. 26-30. CCR5 binder screening and pharmacological characterization. Supplementary Figs. 31-32. OXTR binder identification and pharmacological characterization. Supplementary Figs. 33-39. Structural and functional characterization of CXCR4 maxibinder dCX1_001. Supplementary Figs. 40-47. Yeast display, biophysical and pharmacological characterization of GLP1R, GIPR, and GCGR binders. Supplementary Figs. 48-49. OPS-RD-based PTH1R binder identification and functional screening. Supplementary Figs. 50-60. CGRPR miniprotein in vitro and in vivo characterization. Supplementary Figs. 61-65. Structural analysis of CGRPR-miniprotein complexes and receptor mutagenesis. Supplementary Figs. 66-69. Computational analysis of design methods, in vivo studies of dCX1_001 and surface analysis of chemokine receptors. Supplementary Table 1. OPS-RD assay performance parameters across GFP-tagged GPCRs. Supplementary Tables 2-3. Yeast display and OPS-RD binding data of PAC1R binders. Supplementary Tabes 4-5. Pharmacological data of MRGPRX1 and NK1R binders. Supplementary Table 6. Contact list between MRGPRX1 and adducts at distance of < 5 Å. Supplementary Tables 7-8. Pharmacological data of CXCR4, CCR5, OXTR, GIPR and PTH1R binders. Supplementary Table 9. Binding data of PTH1R binders. Supplementary Tables 10-11. Pharmacological data of CGRPR binders and sequences of functional GPCR binders extensively characterized in pharmacological assays.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in the de novo design of miniproteins targeting G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs), which are crucial drug targets. This advancement in protein design and pharmacological characterization has broad implications for developing new therapeutics, potentially benefiting many patients with various diseases. The detailed supplementary data indicates substantial substance and rigor in the research.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create tiny proteins to target important cell receptors
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – G protein-coupled receptors, or GPCRs, are proteins found on the surface of many cells. They play a key role in how cells respond to signals and are common targets for medicines. Designing new proteins that can interact with these receptors could help develop better drugs.

**What happened** – Researchers have successfully designed very small proteins, called miniproteins, from scratch to bind specifically to different GPCRs. Using computer models and lab tests, they created and tested many miniproteins to find ones that attach well to these receptors. They studied several types of GPCRs and confirmed their designs with detailed experiments, including advanced imaging.

**Impact** – This work is important because it shows a new way to create tiny proteins that can precisely target GPCRs. Since these receptors are involved in many diseases, these miniproteins could lead to new treatments that are more effective and have fewer side effects. The research also provides a strong foundation for future protein design efforts.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue improving these miniproteins and test them in living organisms to see how well they work as medicines. They may also explore designing miniproteins for other types of receptors, expanding the range of possible treatments.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Researchers have designed tiny proteins that can specifically stick to important cell receptors, opening new paths for developing better medicines.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent de minuscules protéines pour cibler des récepteurs cellulaires importants
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les récepteurs couplés aux protéines G, ou GPCR, sont des protéines situées à la surface de nombreuses cellules. Ils jouent un rôle clé dans la façon dont les cellules répondent aux signaux et sont souvent ciblés par des médicaments. Concevoir de nouvelles protéines capables d’interagir avec ces récepteurs pourrait aider à développer de meilleurs traitements.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont réussi à concevoir de très petites protéines, appelées miniprotéines, à partir de zéro, pour se lier spécifiquement à différents GPCR. Grâce à des modèles informatiques et des tests en laboratoire, ils ont créé et testé de nombreuses miniprotéines pour trouver celles qui adhèrent bien à ces récepteurs. Ils ont étudié plusieurs types de GPCR et confirmé leurs conceptions par des expériences détaillées, y compris des images avancées.

**Impact** – Ce travail est important car il montre une nouvelle méthode pour créer de minuscules protéines capables de cibler précisément les GPCR. Comme ces récepteurs sont impliqués dans de nombreuses maladies, ces miniprotéines pourraient conduire à de nouveaux traitements plus efficaces et avec moins d’effets secondaires. La recherche offre aussi une base solide pour de futurs efforts de conception de protéines.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont continuer à améliorer ces miniprotéines et les tester chez des organismes vivants pour vérifier leur efficacité comme médicaments. Ils pourraient aussi explorer la conception de miniprotéines pour d’autres types de récepteurs, élargissant ainsi la gamme de traitements possibles.

**Phrase clé** – Des chercheurs ont conçu de minuscules protéines capables de se fixer spécifiquement à des récepteurs cellulaires importants, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour développer de meilleurs médicaments.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean miniproteínas para atacar receptores celulares importantes
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los receptores acoplados a proteínas G, o GPCR, son proteínas que se encuentran en la superficie de muchas células. Tienen un papel clave en cómo las células responden a señales y son objetivos comunes para medicamentos. Diseñar nuevas proteínas que puedan interactuar con estos receptores podría ayudar a crear mejores tratamientos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores diseñaron con éxito proteínas muy pequeñas, llamadas miniproteínas, desde cero para unirse específicamente a diferentes GPCR. Usaron modelos por computadora y pruebas en laboratorio para crear y evaluar muchas miniproteínas y encontrar las que se adhieren bien a estos receptores. Estudiaron varios tipos de GPCR y confirmaron sus diseños con experimentos detallados, incluyendo imágenes avanzadas.

**Impacto** – Este trabajo es importante porque muestra una nueva forma de crear miniproteínas que pueden dirigirse con precisión a los GPCR. Como estos receptores están involucrados en muchas enfermedades, estas miniproteínas podrían llevar a nuevos tratamientos más efectivos y con menos efectos secundarios. La investigación también ofrece una base sólida para futuros diseños de proteínas.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán mejorando estas miniproteínas y las probarán en organismos vivos para ver qué tan bien funcionan como medicamentos. También podrían explorar diseñar miniproteínas para otros tipos de receptores, ampliando la gama de posibles tratamientos.

**Frase clave** – Investigadores han diseñado miniproteínas que se adhieren específicamente a receptores celulares importantes, abriendo nuevos caminos para desarrollar mejores medicamentos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned illustration of intricately folded miniature protein-like structures gently interacting and fitting precisely into stylized, abstracted cellular receptor shapes resembling elegant, curved locks, set against a soft, natural background of subtle pastel hues.

articles.title: How we’re using AI tools to improve psychedelic-drug research
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 21 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01467-yFélix Schoeller’s team built a realistic artificial-intelligence chatbot to train facilitators needed for research into psychoactive drugs — and, ultimately, to improve public health.
articles.content:
LSD and other psychedelic substances are in clinical trials for use as treatments for various mental-health conditions.Credit: Alfred Pasieka/SPL

When cognitive scientist Félix Schoeller met Joshua White, the founder of a psychedelic support phone line, at a conference in 2024, they immediately hit it off. The two developed an idea for an artificial-intelligence-powered tool called Lucy, to improve training for people administering psychedelic-assisted therapy. Trained facilitators are needed to assist individuals who are undergoing psychedelic therapy for mental-health conditions.

Several psychedelic drugs, including psilocybin, MDMA (also known as ecstasy), ibogaine and LSD, are in clinical trials for conditions ranging from post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) and depression to substance-use disorders. Rigorous clinical trials require trained professionals to administer these drugs and oversee hours-long therapy sessions that are often emotionally challenging for both client and facilitator.

As treatments that use psychedelic drugs gain further clinical approval, and as more US states consider legalizing the use of psychoactive substances for therapy purposes, the demand for facilitators is likely to increase. Today, adults in Oregon and Colorado can legally consume psilocybin if supervised by licensed facilitators, and New Mexico plans to launch a programme for the medical use of psilocybin this year. Yet, there is a global shortage of mental-health providers, and this is likely to be even more severe in psychedelic-assisted therapy because it is an emerging field that requires specialized training.

Schoeller discusses the approach to designing Lucy, how the AI platform is revealing what effective psychological support looks like and how data from Lucy could inform public-health policies around drug regulation.

What inspired Lucy?

Joshua told me about his non-profit organization Fireside Project, which operates a free support phone line, run by trained volunteers, for people seeking guidance during their psychedelic experiences. Fireside Project records its calls for training purposes, and its database contains thousands of support sessions.

Moreover, it’s a high-quality database. After the calls are recorded and anonymized, the volunteers document the techniques that they used during the conversation, such as reflective listening and crisis de-escalation; the drugs and dosages that the callers took; any adverse events that the callers were experiencing; and the mental-health conditions that the callers were trying to address by taking psychedelics.

I immediately understood it to be an incredible epidemiological data set on the consumption of psychedelics in the United States. Joshua and I came up with the idea of using the database to create Lucy and improve training for psychedelic-assisted therapy.

What training issues does Lucy address?

Many training programmes are based on role play, so they are limited by the instructors’ real-life experiences with sessions that involve psychedelic-assisted therapy. Built on thousands of calls with real people, Lucy can present trainees with a range of diverse scenarios and create AI ‘clients’ that speak and sound like an actual person in an altered state of consciousness. Because Lucy is web-based, trainees can practise easily. The format might lower costs and help to scale up the number of people who can be offered training.

How did your team approach developing Lucy?

We analysed Fireside Project’s recordings and caller surveys to understand which techniques were most effective. We also reviewed 29 existing training programmes — including ones used in clinical trials of psychedelic treatments and at services facilitating psilocybin use in Oregon and Colorado — and compared the methods that they used with what we saw in the database.

Then, we designed a scenario builder in the Lucy platform that allows users to tailor the simulated scenarios to their needs. For example, trainees could choose whether they want to practise various skills or learn one skill very well that could be used across drugs and mental-health conditions. They could select to speak with an individual who is using psilocybin for depression, for example, or someone with PTSD taking MDMA. Facilitators will have vastly different experiences depending on the client’s circumstances.

My fight to unlock cannabis and psychedelic drugs for use in medical research

Once the trainees make their selections, large-language models (LLMs) running in the background on the Lucy platform search the repository of Fireside Project transcripts to create a realistic voice model that trainees can role play with. After the simulation, LLMs also analyse the training session’s transcript so that Lucy can offer feedback.

The pilot version is being tested by 400 clinicians, researchers, social workers and educators. Through Lucy’s simulations, they can get a feel for the unusual cognitive and emotional presentations that can occur when a person has taken psychedelic drugs. These include confusion, outbursts, anger and cognitive looping (when someone becomes fixated on one thing). The goal is to provide trainees with many scenarios so that they will feel confident and know how to respond during an actual session.

How might Lucy improve psychedelic research?

Usually in clinical trials of psychedelic treatments, researchers design training courses for facilitators from scratch for the purpose of a single study. Just as drugs and dosing are standardized during a clinical trial, Lucy might help to standardize training, which could make it possible to compare data on safety and outcomes across studies.

One of the scientists Fireside Project is collaborating with is trauma researcher Rachel Yehuda at the Parsons Research Center for Psychedelic Healing in New York City. Yehuda is designing a course for therapists who are administering MDMA to people with PTSD, and Fireside Project is creating simulation modules to complement the course’s instructions on role play. The modules involve various scenarios that therapists might encounter while assisting people before, during and after taking MDMA.

Cognitive scientist Félix Schoeller helped to design and develop an artificial-intelligence tool to train people to facilitate psychedelic-assisted therapy.Credit: Courtesy of Felix Schoeller

Because facilitators must be capable of regulating their own emotions before they can help people who are experiencing an emotional crisis effectively, Lucy’s feedback assessments could improve research. For example, Lucy could flag obstacles to effective facilitation, such as signs of depression and empathy burnout in therapists.

What are you learning from the database?

The Fireside Project data set is giving good indications about effective ways to do psychedelic-assisted therapy. We’ve built a machine-learning model into the Lucy platform that can predict which interactions will result in positive events, such as psychological insights and emotional breakthroughs, or, on the flip side, lead to medical emergencies.

The most important factor for helping someone to manage their stress is genuine human connection. You can say all of the right things, but if you don’t really mean them, and you don’t engage, it doesn’t matter. Trying to measure that is an important challenge, because knowing how best to connect with a client will help in training facilitators to truly empathize with others.
articles.reasoning: The article describes the development of an AI tool, Lucy, that improves training for facilitators of psychedelic-assisted therapy, which is an emerging and promising treatment for mental health conditions. This innovation addresses a critical shortage of trained professionals, has the potential to standardize training across clinical trials, and ultimately improves public health outcomes on a broad scale. The article provides detailed context about the tool's development, its data sources, and its expected impact, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news with significant real-world benefits.
articles.personality_title: AI tool Lucy helps train therapists for psychedelic-assisted mental health care
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Psychedelic drugs like psilocybin and MDMA are being tested to treat mental health problems such as PTSD and depression. These treatments require trained facilitators to support patients during therapy sessions, but there aren’t enough trained people to meet the growing demand.

**What happened** – Félix Schoeller and Joshua White created an AI chatbot named Lucy to train facilitators. Lucy uses thousands of recorded support calls from the Fireside Project, a psychedelic support phone line, to simulate real-life therapy situations. Trainees can practice handling different challenges they might face during psychedelic therapy in a safe, realistic way.

**Impact** – Lucy offers a new way to train more facilitators at lower cost and with more variety in scenarios than traditional role-playing. This can help prepare workers for the emotional and cognitive effects patients may experience. The tool also helps standardize training across studies, making it easier to compare results and improve safety in psychedelic therapy.

**What's next step** – The pilot version of Lucy is being tested by 400 health professionals. Researchers plan to expand its use to support training for different drugs and mental health conditions. Data from Lucy may also guide future public health policies on psychedelic drug use.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Lucy is an AI tool that uses real support call data to better train facilitators for psychedelic therapy, helping meet growing demand and improve mental health care.
articles.personality_title_fr: Lucy, un outil d’IA pour former les thérapeutes en soins psychédéliques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des drogues psychédéliques comme la psilocybine et la MDMA sont testées pour traiter des problèmes de santé mentale tels que le SSPT et la dépression. Ces traitements nécessitent des facilitateurs formés pour accompagner les patients, mais il y en a trop peu pour répondre à la demande croissante.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Félix Schoeller et Joshua White ont créé un chatbot d’IA nommé Lucy pour former ces facilitateurs. Lucy utilise des milliers d’enregistrements d’appels de soutien du Fireside Project, une ligne d’aide psychédélique, pour simuler des situations réelles de thérapie. Les stagiaires peuvent s’exercer à gérer différentes difficultés dans un environnement sûr et réaliste.

**Impact** – Lucy offre une nouvelle méthode pour former plus de facilitateurs à moindre coût et avec plus de diversité que les jeux de rôle traditionnels. Cela aide à préparer les professionnels aux effets émotionnels et cognitifs que les patients peuvent vivre. L’outil aide aussi à standardiser la formation entre les études, facilitant la comparaison des résultats et la sécurité.

**Prochaine étape** – La version pilote de Lucy est testée par 400 professionnels de santé. Les chercheurs prévoient d’élargir son usage pour différents médicaments et troubles mentaux. Les données recueillies pourraient aussi orienter les politiques de santé publique sur l’usage des psychédéliques.

**Phrase clé** – Lucy est un outil d’IA utilisant des données réelles pour mieux former les facilitateurs en thérapie psychédélique, répondant à la demande croissante et améliorant les soins de santé mentale.
articles.personality_title_es: Lucy, una herramienta de IA para formar terapeutas en tratamientos psicodélicos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Drogas psicodélicas como la psilocibina y el MDMA se están probando para tratar problemas de salud mental como el TEPT y la depresión. Estos tratamientos requieren facilitadores capacitados para apoyar a los pacientes, pero hay pocos profesionales disponibles para la demanda creciente.

**Qué pasó** – Félix Schoeller y Joshua White crearon un chatbot de inteligencia artificial llamado Lucy para formar a estos facilitadores. Lucy usa miles de llamadas grabadas del Fireside Project, una línea de apoyo psicodélico, para simular situaciones reales de terapia. Los aprendices pueden practicar cómo manejar diferentes desafíos de forma segura y realista.

**Impacto** – Lucy ofrece una forma nueva de entrenar a más facilitadores a menor costo y con más variedad que las prácticas tradicionales. Esto ayuda a preparar a los trabajadores para los efectos emocionales y cognitivos que pueden experimentar los pacientes. La herramienta también estandariza la formación entre estudios, facilitando la comparación de resultados y la seguridad.

**Próximo paso** – La versión piloto de Lucy está siendo probada por 400 profesionales de la salud. Los investigadores planean ampliar su uso para diferentes drogas y condiciones mentales. Los datos de Lucy podrían guiar políticas públicas futuras sobre el uso de psicodélicos.

**Frase clave** – Lucy es una herramienta de IA que utiliza datos reales para mejorar la formación de facilitadores en terapia psicodélica, ayudando a cubrir la demanda creciente y mejorar la atención en salud mental.

articles.title: UN agencies step up Ebola response in eastern DR Congo
articles.summary: United Nations agencies have moved swiftly to support efforts to contain the latest Ebola outbreak in eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC), delivering emergency medical supplies, protective equipment and logistics support.
articles.content:
As health authorities in both the DRC and Uganda respond to the deadly resurgence, the UN is also helping to strengthen preparedness and raise awareness in at-risk communities.

UN aid workers have been on the job since the outbreak was declared on 15 May in Ituri province in eastern DRC.

Tweet URL

Within 72 hours, the World Health Organization (WHO) delivered 11.5 tonnes of medical supplies and equipment from the capital, Kinshasa, and from its regional hubs in Dakar and Nairobi.

The supplies included personal protective equipment, medical kits and tents. Water, sanitation and hygiene items were also provided to help prevent infection and support effective case management.

UN mission support

WHO said collaboration with partner organizations and the private sector has been crucial.

The UN peacekeeping mission in the DRC, known as MONUSCO, quickly deployed its air assets to support the Congolese authorities and UN agencies.

Starting on Sunday, MONUSCO established an “air bridge” to transport WHO emergency supplies from Nairobi to Bunia, capital of Ituri province. Four vehicles and two motorcycles were also sent to the city to strengthen logistic efforts on the ground.

By Wednesday, the mission had airlifted nearly 30 tonnes of supplies to Bunia.

Peacekeepers spread awareness

MONUSCO’s response extends beyond logistics as its peacekeepers are actively conducting community awareness campaigns.

On Monday, the “blue helmets” were in Tchabi, located in Irumi territory some 120 kilometres from Bunia, informing dozens of residents about key preventative measures such as personal hygiene and risks associated with consuming bush meat.

A similar exercise was carried out the following day in Fataki, targeting internally displaced people and local communities.

Teams used loudspeakers to stress the importance of regular handwashing, proper use of protective equipment, and recognising Ebola symptoms.

“These initiatives aim to enhance community preparedness, reduce panic, and protect vulnerable populations, in line with MONUSCO’s civilian protection mandate,” the UN mission said.

Health emergency adds to hunger crisis

The outbreak is compounding an already severe humanitarian situation in eastern DRC, the World Food Programme (WFP) warned, where armed groups have been active for decades.

It comes at a particularly dangerous moment for the country overall as 26.5 million people nationwide are already facing acute food insecurity.

This includes 10 million people in Ituri and three other eastern provinces who are experiencing severe hunger driven by conflict and displacement.

In Ituri alone, some 1.7 million people – more than a third of the population – are at crisis or worse hunger levels.

Delivering vital assistance

“Indeed, the epicentre of the current health crisis is in the heart of WFP’s operational areas of eastern DRC, positioning us to play a key role in supporting the overall response,” the UN agency said.

“Already, we are providing vital assistance across several pillars – including support to frontline health workers to safely carry out response efforts.”

WFP is facilitating the transport of aid workers, medical supplies and essential cargo into hard-to-reach areas and stands ready to scale up efforts.

It is also providing critical logistics services on behalf of the wider humanitarian community, in addition to providing food and nutrition assistance to patients, survivors, contacts and affected households.

WFP currently needs over $214 million to support operations in the DRC, and over $10 million specifically for Ebola response.

Concern for children

Meanwhile, the UN child rights agency UNICEF has voiced concern for children as they “are especially vulnerable to the impacts of Ebola outbreaks, including disruptions to essential services, loss of parents and caregivers, stigma, psychosocial distress, and increased protection risks.”

UNICEF support to date has included mobilising nearly 50 tonnes of supplies that includes disinfectants and soaps, personal protective equipment, water purification tablets and water tanks.

An Emergency Rapid Response team is also on the way to Bunia to provide technical assistance in priority areas, including support for risk communication and community engagement.

Although over 2,000 community health workers are already in place, additional capacity and resources are urgently needed, particularly in hard-to-reach areas.

Rare strain

The DRC has battled Ebola 17 times since the virus was first discovered 50 years ago. The latest outbreak was caused by the rare Bundibugyo strain, and no vaccines or medicines currently exist to treat it.

To date, there have been 139 deaths in the DRC, and some 600 suspected cases, while Uganda has confirmed two cases.

WHO declared that the outbreak is a public health emergency of international concern – requiring countries to coordinate and cooperate on response – but not a pandemic emergency.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant and coordinated international response by UN agencies to contain a deadly Ebola outbreak in eastern DRC, involving delivery of medical supplies, logistics, community awareness, and support for vulnerable populations. This response has broad public health implications, aims to prevent further spread, and supports a region facing compounded humanitarian crises, thus representing a positive real-world impact with substantial substance and significance.
articles.personality_title: UN agencies quickly respond to Ebola outbreak in eastern DR Congo
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – A new Ebola outbreak began on May 15 in Ituri province, eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC). This region is already struggling with hunger and conflict, making the health crisis more difficult to manage.

**What happened** – United Nations agencies, including the World Health Organization (WHO) and UNICEF, immediately sent medical supplies, protective gear, and support teams to help stop the outbreak. The UN peacekeeping mission MONUSCO used planes and vehicles to deliver nearly 30 tonnes of supplies to the affected area. Peacekeepers also visited local communities to teach people how to protect themselves from Ebola.

**Impact** – This quick and organized response helps prevent the virus from spreading further. It also supports health workers and vulnerable groups like children, who face extra risks during outbreaks. The effort is important because the Ebola strain involved is rare and has no vaccine, so stopping it early is critical.

**What's next step** – UN agencies will continue delivering supplies, increasing community awareness, and supporting health teams on the ground. They need more funds to keep up these efforts and reach remote areas. The goal is to control the outbreak while also addressing hunger and other challenges in the region.

**One-sentence takeaway** – UN agencies are working together to deliver fast medical aid and education to control a rare Ebola outbreak amid a difficult humanitarian crisis in eastern DR Congo.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les agences de l’ONU réagissent rapidement à l’épidémie d’Ebola dans l’est de la RDC
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Une nouvelle épidémie d’Ebola a débuté le 15 mai dans la province d’Ituri, dans l’est de la République démocratique du Congo (RDC). Cette région est déjà confrontée à la faim et aux conflits, ce qui complique la gestion de la crise sanitaire.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Les agences des Nations unies, dont l’Organisation mondiale de la santé (OMS) et l’UNICEF, ont immédiatement envoyé des fournitures médicales, des équipements de protection et des équipes de soutien pour aider à contenir l’épidémie. La mission de maintien de la paix de l’ONU, MONUSCO, a utilisé des avions et des véhicules pour livrer près de 30 tonnes de matériel dans la zone touchée. Les casques bleus ont aussi sensibilisé les communautés locales aux mesures de protection contre Ebola.

**Impact** – Cette réponse rapide et organisée aide à empêcher la propagation du virus. Elle soutient également les agents de santé et les groupes vulnérables comme les enfants, qui sont plus exposés pendant les épidémies. Cette action est cruciale car la souche d’Ebola est rare et ne dispose pas de vaccin.

**Prochaine étape** – Les agences de l’ONU continueront à fournir des ressources, à renforcer la sensibilisation et à appuyer les équipes sanitaires sur le terrain. Elles ont besoin de fonds supplémentaires pour maintenir ces efforts et atteindre les zones isolées. L’objectif est de maîtriser l’épidémie tout en répondant aux autres défis comme la faim.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les agences de l’ONU unissent leurs efforts pour apporter rapidement aide médicale et informations afin de lutter contre une épidémie rare d’Ebola au cœur d’une crise humanitaire dans l’est de la RDC.
articles.personality_title_es: Agencias de la ONU responden rápidamente al brote de Ébola en el este del Congo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Un nuevo brote de Ébola comenzó el 15 de mayo en la provincia de Ituri, en el este de la República Democrática del Congo (RDC). Esta región ya enfrenta hambre y conflictos, lo que dificulta controlar la crisis sanitaria.

**Qué pasó** – Las agencias de las Naciones Unidas, como la Organización Mundial de la Salud (OMS) y UNICEF, enviaron rápidamente suministros médicos, equipo de protección y equipos de apoyo para ayudar a contener el brote. La misión de paz de la ONU, MONUSCO, usó aviones y vehículos para entregar casi 30 toneladas de suministros en la zona afectada. Los cascos azules también visitaron comunidades para enseñar cómo protegerse del Ébola.

**Impacto** – Esta respuesta rápida y organizada ayuda a evitar que el virus se propague más. También apoya a los trabajadores de salud y a grupos vulnerables como los niños, que enfrentan riesgos adicionales durante los brotes. La acción es importante porque la cepa de Ébola es rara y no tiene vacuna.

**Próximo paso** – Las agencias de la ONU continuarán entregando suministros, aumentando la conciencia comunitaria y apoyando a los equipos de salud en el terreno. Necesitan más fondos para mantener estos esfuerzos y llegar a áreas remotas. El objetivo es controlar el brote mientras se atienden otros problemas como el hambre.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Las agencias de la ONU trabajan juntas para entregar ayuda médica rápida y educación para controlar un brote raro de Ébola en medio de una crisis humanitaria en el este del Congo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of symbolic blue helmets gently encircling a stylized map of eastern DR Congo with delicate medical supply crates and tents arranged around it, while abstract figures of peacekeepers use loudspeakers depicted as soft glowing waves reaching out to simplified community silhouettes, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones and soft blues.
